text: Sanskrit = sam (together/complete) + krita (made) = "perfectly constructed" You can't understand Hindu philosophy without understanding the language it thinks in. Sanskrit isn't just a vehicle for ideas — it IS the architecture. - Compositional: words built from root sounds (dhatus) + prefixes + suffixes - Panini's Ashtadhyayi (~500 BCE): ~4,000 generative grammar rules — the first formal language specification ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sanskrit Basics — Lesson 1

text: | Short | Long | Transliteration | |---|---|---| | अ | आ | a / ā | | इ | ई | i / ī | | उ | ऊ | u / ū | | ऋ | ॠ | ṛ / ṝ | | ए | ऐ | e / ai | | ओ | औ | o / au | अ (a) is the default sound — every consonant carries it unless told otherwise. क is not "k", it's "ka".

metadata.lesson_title: Sanskrit Basics — Lesson 1

text: Organized by mouth position — throat to lips: | Location | Unvoiced | Voiced | Nasal | |---|---|---|---| | Throat (Kanthya) | क ka, ख kha | ग ga, घ gha | ङ ṅa | | Palate (Talavya) | च cha, छ chha | ज ja, झ jha | ञ ña | | Roof (Murdhanya) | ट ṭa, ठ ṭha | ड ḍa, ढ ḍha | ण ṇa | | Teeth (Dantya) | त ta, थ tha | द da, ध dha | न na | | Lips (Oshthya) | प pa, फ pha | ब ba, भ bha | म ma | Each row: throat → lips. Each column: less energy → more energy → nasal. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sanskrit Basics — Lesson 1

text: | Dhatu | Meaning | Examples | |---|---|---| | भू (bhū) | to be, to become | bhūmi (earth), bhāva (state of being), bhūta (element) | | कृ (kṛ) | to do, to make | karma (action), saṃskṛta (Sanskrit), kṛṣṇa (the dark one) | | गम (gam) | to go | āgama (arrival/scripture), gati (movement/destiny) | | विद (vid) | to know | veda (knowledge), vidyā (learning), avidyā (ignorance) | | वच (vac) | to speak | vāk (speech), vachana (statement) | karma = कृ (to do) + man (suffix) = "the thing that is done" ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sanskrit Basics — Lesson 1

text: Ayurveda = āyus (life) + veda (knowledge) → "knowledge of life" - Yoga = yuj (to yoke/unite) → "union" - Nirvana = nir (without) + vāna (blowing/desire) → "extinguishing" - Guru = gu (darkness) + ru (remover) → "remover of darkness" - Mantra = man (to think) + tra (instrument) → "instrument of thought" ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sanskrit Basics — Lesson 1

text: सन्धि (sandhi) = सम् (sam, together) + धि (dhi, from धा/dhā, to place) → "placing together" When two Sanskrit sounds meet — at the end of one word and the start of the next, or within a compound — they transform. They don't just sit side by side. They merge, shift, or replace each other according to precise rules. This is not optional. This is how the language breathes. > In English: "do not" → "don't" is informal, optional. > In Sanskrit: sandhi is the grammar. The unmerged form is the exception. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sandhi — The Rules of Sound Merging

text: 1. You can't read texts without it — every shloka, every sutra has sandhi applied. If you don't know the rules, you can't split words apart to find their meaning. 2. It reveals how the language thinks — sounds aren't isolated units, they exist in relationship. Context changes identity. 3. It's the original tokenizer — just as an LLM merges subwords into tokens based on context and frequency, Sanskrit merges sounds based on phonetic rules. The boundary between words is fluid. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sandhi — The Rules of Sound Merging

text: | Type | Where it happens | Example | |---|---|---| | स्वरसन्धि (Svara Sandhi) | Vowel + Vowel | Two vowels meet at a word boundary | | व्यञ्जनसन्धि (Vyanjana Sandhi) | Consonant + Consonant/Vowel | A consonant ending meets the next sound | | विसर्गसन्धि (Visarga Sandhi) | Visarga (ः) + next sound | The breath-sound ः transforms | ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sandhi — The Rules of Sound Merging

text: When two similar vowels meet, they combine into the long version. `` अ + अ = आ a + a = ā इ + इ = ई i + i = ī उ + उ = ऊ u + u = ū `` Example: - देव + अलयः = देवालयः - deva + ālayaḥ = devālayaḥ - "god" + "abode" = "temple" The अ at the end of देव and the आ at the start of आलय merge into आ.

metadata.section: Rule 1: Sama (Same) — Like merges with like
metadata.lesson_title: Sandhi — The Rules of Sound Merging

text: When अ/आ meets इ/ई, उ/ऊ, or ऋ/ॠ, they combine into a "guna" vowel. `` अ/आ + इ/ई = ए a + i = e अ/आ + उ/ऊ = ओ a + u = o अ/आ + ऋ/ॠ = अर् a + ṛ = ar `` Example: - महा + ईश्वरः = महेश्वरः - mahā + īśvaraḥ = maheśvaraḥ - "great" + "lord" = "Maheshvara" (name of Shiva) The आ + ई merge into ए.

metadata.section: Rule 2: Guna — Short upgrades to medium
metadata.lesson_title: Sandhi — The Rules of Sound Merging

text: When अ/आ meets ए/ऐ or ओ/औ, they intensify. `` अ/आ + ए/ऐ = ऐ a + e/ai = ai अ/आ + ओ/औ = औ a + o/au = au `` Example: - एक + एक = एकैक - eka + eka = ekaika - "one" + "one" = "each one"

metadata.section: Rule 3: Vriddhi — Short upgrades to strongest
metadata.lesson_title: Sandhi — The Rules of Sound Merging

text: A nasal takes the position of the consonant that follows it. `` Before क/ख/ग/घ → ङ (throat nasal) Before च/छ/ज/झ → ञ (palate nasal) Before ट/ठ/ड/ढ → ण (roof nasal) Before त/थ/द/ध → न (teeth nasal) Before प/फ/ब/भ → म (lip nasal) `` This is why the consonant grid from Lesson 1 matters — sandhi follows the same map. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sandhi — The Rules of Sound Merging

text: The विसर्ग (visarga, ः) is a soft breath at the end of a word. It's unstable — it transforms based on what follows. `` Before voiced consonant/vowel: ः often → ओ or drops Before क/ख: ः stays or becomes स् Before च/छ: ः becomes श् Before ट/ठ: ः becomes ष् Before त/थ: ः becomes स् `` Example: - रामः + च = रामश्च - rāmaḥ + ca = rāmaśca - "Rama" + "and" = "and Rama" ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sandhi — The Rules of Sound Merging

text: सत्यमेव जयते (satyameva jayate) — "Truth alone triumphs" India's national motto. Let's reverse the sandhi: `` सत्यम् + एव → सत्यमेव satyam + eva → satyameva `` The म् (m) before ए (e, a vowel) stays as म according to sandhi rules, and the words fuse. - सत्य (satya) = truth — from root सत् (sat, being/existence) - एव (eva) = alone, indeed - जयते (jayate) = triumphs — from root जि (ji, to conquer) > From the Mundaka Upanishad (3.1.6): > सत्यमेव जयते नानृतम् > "Truth alone triumphs, not untruth" ---

metadata.section: Sandhi in Action: Deconstructing a Famous Phrase
metadata.lesson_title: Sandhi — The Rules of Sound Merging

text: Try to find the word boundary and identify which sandhi rule was applied: | Merged Form | Split Into | Rule | |---|---|---| | हिमालयः (himālayaḥ) | हिम + आलयः (hima + ālayaḥ, snow + abode) | अ + आ = आ (sama) | | सूर्योदयः (sūryodayaḥ) | सूर्य + उदयः (sūrya + udayaḥ, sun + rising) | अ + उ = ओ (guna) | | महर्षिः (maharṣiḥ) | महा + ऋषिः (mahā + ṛṣiḥ, great + sage) | आ + ऋ = अर् (guna) | | नरेन्द्रः (narendraḥ) | नर + इन्द्रः (nara + indraḥ, man + lord) | अ + इ = ए (guna) | | रामश्च (rāmaśca) | रामः + च (rāmaḥ + ca, Rama + and) | visarga before च | ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sandhi — The Rules of Sound Merging

text: विभक्ति (vibhakti) = वि (vi, apart/distinct) + भक्ति (bhakti, from भज्/bhaj, to divide/share) → "that which distinguishes" In English, word order tells you who did what: - "Rama killed Ravana" ≠ "Ravana killed Rama" In Sanskrit, word order doesn't matter. Instead, every noun gets a suffix — a vibhakti — that declares its role in the sentence. The noun carries its own job description. `` रामः रावणं हन्ति = Rama kills Ravana रावणं रामः हन्ति = Rama kills Ravana हन्ति रावणं रामः = Rama kills Ravana `` All three are identical in meaning. रामः (with ः) is always the doer. रावणं (with ं) is always the one acted upon. No ambiguity, any order. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Vibhakti — The Eight Cases

text: 1. Poetry becomes free — shlokas can arrange words for rhythm and beauty, not grammar 2. Precision without rigidity — each word is self-describing 3. It's how attention works — in a transformer, position encoding + attention lets any token relate to any other regardless of position. Vibhakti is the original position-independent encoding. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Vibhakti — The Eight Cases

text: | # | Name | Sanskrit | Question it Answers | English Equivalent | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Prathama | प्रथमा | Who/what? (doer) | Nominative — subject | | 2 | Dvitiya | द्वितीया | Whom/what? (receiver) | Accusative — object | | 3 | Tritiya | तृतीया | By whom? With what? | Instrumental — by/with | | 4 | Chaturthi | चतुर्थी | For whom? To whom? | Dative — for/to | | 5 | Panchami | पञ्चमी | From where? Because of? | Ablative — from | | 6 | Shashthi | षष्ठी | Whose? Of what? | Genitive — of/possessive | | 7 | Saptami | सप्तमी | Where? When? In what? | Locative — in/on/at | | 8 | Sambodhana | सम्बोधन | O...! Hey...! | Vocative — direct address | > Notice: the case names are simply the Sanskrit ordinal numbers — first, second, third... The language names its grammar with numbers, not labels. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Vibhakti — The Eight Cases

text: Let's decline one word through all 8 cases (masculine, singular): | # | Case | Form | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Prathama | रामः (rāmaḥ) | Rama (as subject) | | 2 | Dvitiya | रामम् (rāmam) | Rama (as object) | | 3 | Tritiya | रामेण (rāmeṇa) | by/with Rama | | 4 | Chaturthi | रामाय (rāmāya) | for/to Rama | | 5 | Panchami | रामात् (rāmāt) | from Rama | | 6 | Shashthi | रामस्य (rāmasya) | of Rama / Rama's | | 7 | Saptami | रामे (rāme) | in/on/at Rama | | 8 | Sambodhana | हे राम! (he rāma!) | O Rama! | The root राम never changes. Only the suffix shifts. The word announces its own role. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Vibhakti — The Eight Cases

text: Behind the 8 grammatical cases, Panini defined 6 karakas — the actual semantic roles in an action. This is the deeper layer. | Karaka | Sanskrit | Role | Maps to Case | |---|---|---|---| | Karta | कर्ता | The doer | Prathama (1) | | Karma | कर्म | The thing done upon | Dvitiya (2) | | Karana | करण | The instrument | Tritiya (3) | | Sampradana | सम्प्रदान | The recipient | Chaturthi (4) | | Apadana | अपादान | The source/origin | Panchami (5) | | Adhikarana | अधिकरण | The location/context | Saptami (7) | > Shashthi (6, possessive) and Sambodhana (8, address) don't have karakas — they describe relationship and attention, not action roles. All 6 karakas derive from the root कृ (kṛ, to do) — the same root that gives us karma. Every role in a sentence is defined by its relationship to the action. ---

metadata.section: Each Case Has a Karaka (Semantic Role)
metadata.lesson_title: Vibhakti — The Eight Cases

text: Let's construct one sentence and see all parts: > रामः वनात् सीतायै बाणेन रावणं हन्ति | Word | Form | Case | Karaka | Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | रामः | rāmaḥ | Prathama (1) | Karta | Rama (doer) | | वनात् | vanāt | Panchami (5) | Apadana | from the forest | | सीतायै | sītāyai | Chaturthi (4) | Sampradana | for Sita | | बाणेन | bāṇena | Tritiya (3) | Karana | with an arrow | | रावणं | rāvaṇam | Dvitiya (2) | Karma | Ravana (object) | | हन्ति | hanti | — | Verb | kills | "Rama, from the forest, for Sita, with an arrow, kills Ravana." Rearrange these words in ANY order — the meaning holds. Each word carries its own tag. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Vibhakti — The Eight Cases

text: Each case has three number forms: | Number | Sanskrit | Example (राम, Prathama) | |---|---|---| | Singular (एकवचन) | ekavachana | रामः (rāmaḥ) — one Rama | | Dual (द्विवचन) | dvivachana | रामौ (rāmau) — two Ramas | | Plural (बहुवचन) | bahuvachana | रामाः (rāmāḥ) — many Ramas | > Sanskrit has a dual number. Not one, not many — exactly two. This matters philosophically: duality (द्वैत, dvaita) gets its own grammatical form. The language treats "twoness" as fundamentally different from "manyness." ---

metadata.lesson_title: Vibhakti — The Eight Cases

text: | Case | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | रामः | रामौ | रामाः | | Dvitiya (2) | रामम् | रामौ | रामान् | | Tritiya (3) | रामेण | रामाभ्याम् | रामैः | | Chaturthi (4) | रामाय | रामाभ्याम् | रामेभ्यः | | Panchami (5) | रामात् | रामाभ्याम् | रामेभ्यः | | Shashthi (6) | रामस्य | रामयोः | रामाणाम् | | Saptami (7) | रामे | रामयोः | रामेषु | | Sambodhana (8) | हे राम | हे रामौ | हे रामाः | > 8 cases × 3 numbers = 24 forms per noun. And this is just one declension pattern. Feminine and neuter nouns have their own tables. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Vibhakti — The Eight Cases

text: | Sentence | Find the Case | |---|---| | गजः चलति (gajaḥ calati) — The elephant walks | गजः = Prathama (doer) | | नरः फलम् खादति (naraḥ phalam khādati) — The man eats a fruit | फलम् = Dvitiya (object) | | बालः हस्तेन लिखति (bālaḥ hastena likhati) — The boy writes with his hand | हस्तेन = Tritiya (instrument) | | नृपः ब्राह्मणाय धनं ददाति (nṛpaḥ brāhmaṇāya dhanaṁ dadāti) — The king gives wealth to the brahmin | ब्राह्मणाय = Chaturthi (recipient) | | वृक्षात् पत्रं पतति (vṛkṣāt patraṁ patati) — The leaf falls from the tree | वृक्षात् = Panchami (source) | | रामस्य गृहम् (rāmasya gṛham) — Rama's house | रामस्य = Shashthi (possessive) | | जलम् नद्यां वहति (jalam nadyāṁ vahati) — Water flows in the river | नद्यां = Saptami (location) | ---

metadata.lesson_title: Vibhakti — The Eight Cases

text: In Sanskrit, every noun is self-addressed. It carries metadata about its role. No external parser (word order) needed. This is: - Self-describing data — like JSON where each field carries its own key - Position-independent encoding — like transformer attention where any token attends to any other - Role-based identity — you are not defined by where you sit, but by the suffix you carry > The word विभक्ति itself tells you: identity comes through distinction. You know what something IS by how it's been divided from everything else. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Vibhakti — The Eight Cases

text: लकार (lakāra) = the system of 10 verb forms in Sanskrit grammar. The name comes from Panini's metalanguage: each form is designated by a marker starting with ल (la), so they're collectively called lakaras. Panini didn't name them "tenses" or "moods" — he gave them algebraic labels. The verb forms do the rest. Here's the thing: English splits verbs into "tenses" (past, present, future) and "moods" (imperative, subjunctive, optative) as separate systems. Sanskrit unifies them. A lakara encodes time, mood, possibility, command, and wish — all in one conjugation framework. The verb carries everything. `` भवति (bhavati) — he/she/it is (present, indicative, third person, singular) `` That single word encodes: who (third person), how many (singular), when (present), and what kind of statement (indicative fact). No helping verbs. No auxiliary constructions. One word, fully tagged. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: Because reality isn't just past-present-future. Sanskrit verbs must express: - What is happening (fact) - What was happening (recalled past) - What will happen (prediction) - What should happen (command) - What might happen (possibility) - What you wish would happen (benediction) - What happened long ago (remote narrative past) - What happened as witnessed (recent past) - What was about to happen (conditional) - What is general truth (timeless/Vedic present) Ten slots. Ten lakaras. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: | # | Lakara | Sanskrit | Meaning / Usage | Approx. English Equivalent | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Laṭ | लट् | Present tense — what is happening now | Present indicative | | 2 | Liṭ | लिट् | Past — remote, unwitnessed, narrative | Past perfect / "had done" | | 3 | Luṭ | लुट् | Future — periphrastic (analytical) | "will do" (distant/formal future) | | 4 | Lṛṭ | लृट् | Future — simple, direct | "will do" (straightforward future) | | 5 | Leṭ | लेट् | Vedic subjunctive — possibility, exhortation | Subjunctive (archaic, mostly Vedic) | | 6 | Loṭ | लोट् | Command / request | Imperative — "do this!" | | 7 | Laṅ | लङ् | Past — imperfect, recent, witnessed | Past imperfect / "was doing" | | 8 | Liṅ | लिङ् | Wish / potential / should | Optative — "may it be", "one should" | | 9 | Luṅ | लुङ् | Past — aorist, simple completed past | Simple past / "did" | | 10 | Lṛṅ | लृङ् | Conditional — would have, if-then | Conditional — "would have done" | > Notice the pattern in Panini's labels: every marker starts with ल and ends with a nasal or stop. The vowel in the middle distinguishes them. This isn't naming — it's indexing. Panini built a lookup table 2,500 years ago.

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: Time-based (indicative): - लट् (Laṭ) — present - लङ् (Laṅ) — recent past - लुङ् (Luṅ) — completed past (aorist) - लिट् (Liṭ) — remote past (perfect) - लृट् (Lṛṭ) — simple future - लुट् (Luṭ) — distant future (periphrastic) Mood-based (non-indicative): - लोट् (Loṭ) — imperative (command) - लिङ् (Liṅ) — optative (wish/potential) - लेट् (Leṭ) — subjunctive (Vedic only) - लृङ् (Lṛṅ) — conditional (hypothetical) ---

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: What is happening, what is generally true. This is the workhorse. When you see a verb in most Sanskrit prose, it's probably Laṭ. Formation: dhatu + vikarana (class marker) + tiṅ pratyaya (personal ending) The verb gets built in layers: `` भू → भव (dhatu strengthened) → भव + अ (vikarana for class 1) → भवति (+ ti ending for 3rd person singular) ``

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: What was happening, what happened (recently, witnessed). This is the most common past tense in classical Sanskrit. It describes completed action in the past — think of it as narrative past. Formation: augment अ (a-) + dhatu + vikarana + modified endings The distinguishing feature is the augment — a prefix अ (a-) added before the verb stem: `` भवति (bhavati, "is") → अभवत् (abhavat, "was") `` That prefixed अ is the marker of past tense. You'll learn to spot it instantly.

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: What will happen. Formation: dhatu + स्य (sya) or इष्य (iṣya) + personal endings The future is marked by the infix स्य inserted between the stem and the ending: `` भवति (bhavati, "is") → भविष्यति (bhaviṣyati, "will be") `` The ष्य (ṣya) in the middle is the future flag. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: Before we conjugate, one essential distinction. Every Sanskrit dhatu can take one of two sets of endings: | | Parasmaipada (परस्मैपद) | Atmanepada (आत्मनेपद) | |---|---|---| | Root meaning | परस्मै (parasmai) = for another + पद (pada) = word/form | आत्मने (ātmane) = for oneself + पद (pada) = word/form | | Sense | Action whose fruit goes to others | Action whose fruit comes to oneself | | Example | पचति (pacati) — he cooks (for others) | पचते (pacate) — he cooks (for himself) | This is not just grammar — it encodes directionality of benefit. The verb form itself tells you who the action serves. Some dhatus take only parasmaipada, some only atmanepada, and some take both (उभयपदी, ubhayapadī). भू (bhū) is parasmaipada. Verbs like लभ् (labh, to obtain) are atmanepada — because the fruit of obtaining naturally comes to the obtainer. > This is a genuine parallel to distributed systems: is this function call fire-and-forget (parasmaipada — action radiates outward) or does it return a value to the caller (atmanepada — result comes back to self)? ---

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: Every conjugated verb encodes person and number: | | Sanskrit Term | Who | |---|---|---| | Third person | प्रथम पुरुष (prathama puruṣa) | He/she/it/they | | Second person | मध्यम पुरुष (madhyama puruṣa) | You | | First person | उत्तम पुरुष (uttama puruṣa) | I/we | > Note: Sanskrit counts persons in reverse order compared to English. Third person is "first" (prathama) because in traditional grammar, the subject being discussed comes first — the speaker comes last. Grammar isn't about you. You are the last reference frame. Each person has three numbers: singular (एकवचन), dual (द्विवचन), plural (बहुवचन). That gives us a 3 × 3 grid = 9 forms per lakara per pada.

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: | Person | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (3rd) | -ते (-te) | -ते (-ete) | -न्ते (-nte) | | Madhyama (2nd) | -से (-se) | -एथे (-ethe) | -ध्वे (-dhve) | | Uttama (1st) | -ए (-e) | -वहे (-vahe) | -महे (-mahe) | ---

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: Dhatu: भू → Strengthened stem: भव (bhava) | Person | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (3rd) | भवति (bhavati) — he/she is | भवतः (bhavataḥ) — those two are | भवन्ति (bhavanti) — they are | | Madhyama (2nd) | भवसि (bhavasi) — you are | भवथः (bhavathaḥ) — you two are | भवथ (bhavatha) — you all are | | Uttama (1st) | भवामि (bhavāmi) — I am | भवावः (bhavāvaḥ) — we two are | भवामः (bhavāmaḥ) — we are | > भवामि — "I am." Two syllables. No subject pronoun needed. Compare with English "I am" — two separate words, one for the subject, one for the verb. Sanskrit packs both into one unit.

metadata.section: भू (bhū) — to be, to become [Class 1, Parasmaipada]
metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: Dhatu: गम् → Strengthened stem: गच्छ (gaccha) (The transformation गम् → गच्छ involves a process called samprasarana and reduplication patterns specific to this dhatu. You'll encounter such stem changes — they're irregular but frequent enough to memorize.) | Person | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (3rd) | गच्छति (gacchati) — he/she goes | गच्छतः (gacchataḥ) — those two go | गच्छन्ति (gacchanti) — they go | | Madhyama (2nd) | गच्छसि (gacchasi) — you go | गच्छथः (gacchathaḥ) — you two go | गच्छथ (gacchatha) — you all go | | Uttama (1st) | गच्छामि (gacchāmi) — I go | गच्छावः (gacchāvaḥ) — we two go | गच्छामः (gacchāmaḥ) — we go |

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: Dhatu: लभ् → Stem: लभ (labha) This one takes atmanepada endings — because obtaining is inherently self-directed. | Person | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (3rd) | लभते (labhate) — he obtains | लभेते (labhete) — those two obtain | लभन्ते (labhante) — they obtain | | Madhyama (2nd) | लभसे (labhase) — you obtain | लभेथे (labhethe) — you two obtain | लभध्वे (labhadhve) — you all obtain | | Uttama (1st) | लभे (labhe) — I obtain | लभावहे (labhāvahe) — we two obtain | लभामहे (labhāmahe) — we obtain | > Compare the endings: भवति vs लभते, भवन्ति vs लभन्ते, भवामि vs लभे. The parasmaipada endings are harder, more outward-sounding (ti, nti, mi). The atmanepada endings are softer, more reflexive (te, nte, e). The sound itself mirrors the direction. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: Formation: अ (augment) + भव (stem) + past endings | Person | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (3rd) | अभवत् (abhavat) — he/she was | अभवताम् (abhavatām) — those two were | अभवन् (abhavan) — they were | | Madhyama (2nd) | अभवः (abhavaḥ) — you were | अभवतम् (abhavatam) — you two were | अभवत (abhavata) — you all were | | Uttama (1st) | अभवम् (abhavam) — I was | अभवाव (abhavāva) — we two were | अभवाम (abhavāma) — we were |

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: | Person | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (3rd) | अगच्छत् (agacchat) — he/she went | अगच्छताम् (agacchatām) — those two went | अगच्छन् (agacchan) — they went | | Madhyama (2nd) | अगच्छः (agacchaḥ) — you went | अगच्छतम् (agacchatam) — you two went | अगच्छत (agacchata) — you all went | | Uttama (1st) | अगच्छम् (agaccham) — I went | अगच्छाव (agacchāva) — we two went | अगच्छाम (agacchāma) — we went | > Spot the pattern: every Laṅ form starts with अ (a-). This augment is the single most reliable marker of past tense. When you're reading a text and see a verb starting with अ that doesn't normally start with अ — it's past tense. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: Formation: भव (stem) + इष्य (iṣya) + endings | Person | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (3rd) | भविष्यति (bhaviṣyati) — will be | भविष्यतः (bhaviṣyataḥ) — those two will be | भविष्यन्ति (bhaviṣyanti) — they will be | | Madhyama (2nd) | भविष्यसि (bhaviṣyasi) — you will be | भविष्यथः (bhaviṣyathaḥ) — you two will be | भविष्यथ (bhaviṣyatha) — you all will be | | Uttama (1st) | भविष्यामि (bhaviṣyāmi) — I will be | भविष्यावः (bhaviṣyāvaḥ) — we two will be | भविष्यामः (bhaviṣyāmaḥ) — we will be |

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: Formation: गम (stem) + इष्य (iṣya) + endings | Person | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (3rd) | गमिष्यति (gamiṣyati) — will go | गमिष्यतः (gamiṣyataḥ) — those two will go | गमिष्यन्ति (gamiṣyanti) — they will go | | Madhyama (2nd) | गमिष्यसि (gamiṣyasi) — you will go | गमिष्यथः (gamiṣyathaḥ) — you two will go | गमिष्यथ (gamiṣyatha) — you all will go | | Uttama (1st) | गमिष्यामि (gamiṣyāmi) — I will go | गमिष्यावः (gamiṣyāvaḥ) — we two will go | गमिष्यामः (gamiṣyāmaḥ) — we will go | > The future infix ष्य (ṣya) is consistent across both. Once you hear it, you know the verb is talking about what hasn't happened yet. The stem may change (भव → भवि, गम → गमि) but the marker doesn't. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: भू (bhū) — third person singular: | Lakara | Form | Meaning | Marker | |---|---|---|---| | लट् (present) | भवति (bhavati) | he is | base form | | लङ् (past) | अभवत् (abhavat) | he was | अ- prefix | | लृट् (future) | भविष्यति (bhaviṣyati) | he will be | -ष्य- infix | गम् (gam) — third person singular: | Lakara | Form | Meaning | Marker | |---|---|---|---| | लट् (present) | गच्छति (gacchati) | he goes | base form | | लङ् (past) | अगच्छत् (agacchat) | he went | अ- prefix | | लृट् (future) | गमिष्यति (gamiṣyati) | he will go | -ष्य- infix | Two markers to internalize: अ- (prefix) = past. -ष्य- (infix) = future. Present is unmarked — it's the default. ---

metadata.section: Quick Reference: One Dhatu Across Three Times
metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: अभविष्यत् (abhaviṣyat) — "would have been" Combines the past augment अ- with the future marker ष्य — literally "past-of-the-future." Used in counterfactuals: "If X had happened, then Y would have happened." > The conditional is architecturally beautiful: it grafts the past marker onto the future stem. "The past version of a future" = what would have been. The grammar is the logic.

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: Try these before looking at the answers. | # | Sanskrit | Hint | |---|---|---| | 1 | अहं गच्छामि (ahaṁ gacchāmi) | अहम् = I; Laṭ form | | 2 | त्वं भवसि (tvaṁ bhavasi) | त्वम् = you; Laṭ form | | 3 | सः अगच्छत् (saḥ agacchat) | सः = he; spot the अ- | | 4 | ते भविष्यन्ति (te bhaviṣyanti) | ते = they; spot the ष्य | | 5 | बालः गृहं गच्छति (bālaḥ gṛhaṁ gacchati) | बालः = boy; गृहम् = house | | 6 | नराः वनम् अगच्छन् (narāḥ vanam agacchan) | नराः = men; वनम् = forest | | 7 | सा फलं लभते (sā phalaṁ labhate) | सा = she; फलम् = fruit; note the ending | | 8 | गच्छ (gaccha) | Just the verb — which lakara? |

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: | # | Translation | Analysis | |---|---|---| | 1 | I go. | Laṭ, uttama puruṣa, ekavachana | | 2 | You are. | Laṭ, madhyama puruṣa, ekavachana | | 3 | He went. | Laṅ (past — see the अ- augment), prathama puruṣa, ekavachana | | 4 | They will be. | Lṛṭ (future — see the ष्य infix), prathama puruṣa, bahuvachana | | 5 | The boy goes to the house. | Laṭ; बालः = nominative (doer), गृहं = accusative (destination) | | 6 | The men went to the forest. | Laṅ; नराः = nominative plural, वनम् = accusative | | 7 | She obtains a fruit. | Laṭ, but atmanepada — notice लभते not लभति. The obtaining is for herself. | | 8 | Go! | Loṭ (imperative), madhyama puruṣa, ekavachana. A command. | ---

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: One more structural piece: not all dhatus conjugate the same way. Panini organized them into 10 classes (gaṇas), each with its own vikarana (class marker inserted between the stem and ending). | Class | Name | Vikarana | Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Bhvādi | -अ (-a) | भू → भव + अ → भवति | | 2 | Adādi | (none) | अद् → अत्ति (atti, eats) | | 3 | Juhotyādi | reduplication | हु → जुहोति (juhoti, offers) | | 4 | Divādi | -य (-ya) | दिव् → दीव्यति (dīvyati, plays) | | 5 | Svādi | -नु (-nu) | सु → सुनोति (sunoti, presses) | | 6 | Tudādi | -अ (-a) | तुद् → तुदति (tudati, strikes) | | 7 | Rudhādi | -न- (-na- infix) | रुध् → रुणद्धि (ruṇaddhi, obstructs) | | 8 | Tanādi | -उ (-u) | तन् → तनोति (tanoti, stretches) | | 9 | Kryādi | -ना (-nā) | क्री → क्रीणाति (krīṇāti, buys) | | 10 | Churādi | -अय (-aya) | चुर् → चोरयति (corayati, steals) | > Each class name follows Panini's convention: it's named after the first dhatu in that class's list. Bhvādi = "starting with भू." This is like naming an array by its first element — a convention, not a description. For now, Classes 1 and 10 are the most regular. Classes 2, 3, and 7 are the ones that will trip you up — they involve stem changes and infixes that break the simple pattern. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: Sanskrit verbs are self-contained instruction packets. A single conjugated form tells you: 1. Who is acting (person — 3rd, 2nd, 1st) 2. How many are acting (number — one, two, many) 3. When the action occurs (time — present, past, future) 4. What kind of statement it is (mood — fact, command, wish, hypothetical) 5. Who benefits from the action (pada — others or self) No subject pronoun required. No auxiliary verbs. No word order dependency. The 10 lakaras are not 10 tenses. They're 10 modes of relating to action across time and intention. English needs helper constructions ("would have been going") to express what Sanskrit encodes in a single morphological form. > Think of it this way: a Sanskrit verb is a fully serialized function call. It encodes the caller, the arguments, the temporal context, and the execution mode — all in one token. Deserialization is deterministic. No ambiguity, no parsing required. The 3 markers to burn into memory: - Base form → present (Laṭ) - अ- prefix → past (Laṅ) - -ष्य- infix → future (Lṛṭ) Everything else is built on these. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Lakaras — The Verb System

text: You now know that Sanskrit nouns carry their role (vibhakti), verbs carry their tense and person (lakara), and sounds merge at boundaries (sandhi). Two critical word classes remain before you can construct real sentences: numbers and pronouns. Both behave unlike anything in English. Numbers decline through cases just like nouns. Pronouns encode gender, number, AND case in a single word — no helper words, no word-order dependency. One form does the work of three English words. सङ्ख्या (saṅkhyā) = सम् (sam, together) + ख्या (khyā, to tell/enumerate) → "complete enumeration" सर्वनाम (sarvanāma) = सर्व (sarva, all) + नाम (nāma, name) → "name for all" — a word that stands in for any noun ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: | # | Devanagari | Transliteration | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | एक | eka | From √इ (i, to go)? Debated. Possibly Proto-Indo-European óynos — cognate with Latin "unus", English "one" | | 2 | द्वि | dvi | PIE dwóh₁ — cognate with Latin "duo", English "two", Greek "dyo" | | 3 | त्रि | tri | PIE tréyes — cognate with Latin "tres", English "three" | | 4 | चतुर् | catur | PIE kʷetwóres — cognate with Latin "quattuor", English "four" | | 5 | पञ्चन् | pañcan | PIE pénkʷe — cognate with Latin "quinque", Greek "pente", English "five" | | 6 | षष् | ṣaṣ | PIE swéḱs — cognate with Latin "sex", English "six" | | 7 | सप्तन् | saptan | PIE septḿ̥ — cognate with Latin "septem", English "seven". Nearly identical across 4,000 years. | | 8 | अष्टन् | aṣṭan | PIE oḱtṓw — cognate with Latin "octo", English "eight" | | 9 | नवन् | navan | PIE h₁néwn̥ — cognate with Latin "novem", English "nine". Also related to नव (nava, new)? Coincidence or deep link — scholars disagree. | | 10 | दशन् | daśan | PIE déḱm̥ — cognate with Latin "decem", English "ten", Greek "deka" | > The Indo-European number system is one of the strongest proofs of common linguistic ancestry. Sanskrit saptan and English seven are the same word, separated by millennia.

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: एक takes all three genders and all eight cases, but only exists in singular (obviously). | Case | Masculine | Feminine | Neuter | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | एकः (ekaḥ) | एका (ekā) | एकम् (ekam) | | Dvitiya (2) | एकम् (ekam) | एकाम् (ekām) | एकम् (ekam) | | Tritiya (3) | एकेन (ekena) | एकया (ekayā) | एकेन (ekena) | | Chaturthi (4) | एकस्मै (ekasmai) | एकस्यै (ekasyai) | एकस्मै (ekasmai) | | Panchami (5) | एकस्मात् (ekasmāt) | एकस्याः (ekasyāḥ) | एकस्मात् (ekasmāt) | | Shashthi (6) | एकस्य (ekasya) | एकस्याः (ekasyāḥ) | एकस्य (ekasya) | | Saptami (7) | एकस्मिन् (ekasmin) | एकस्याम् (ekasyām) | एकस्मिन् (ekasmin) | > Notice: एक doesn't follow the regular अ-stem (rāma-type) declension. It follows the pronominal declension — the same pattern as सर्व, तत्, etc. This is your first hint that numbers and pronouns are deeply related in Sanskrit grammar.

metadata.section: एक (eka, one) — Declines Like a Pronoun
metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: द्वि exists only in the dual number (द्विवचन). It still takes three genders. | Case | Masculine | Feminine | Neuter | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | द्वौ (dvau) | द्वे (dve) | द्वे (dve) | | Dvitiya (2) | द्वौ (dvau) | द्वे (dve) | द्वे (dve) | | Tritiya (3) | द्वाभ्याम् (dvābhyām) | द्वाभ्याम् (dvābhyām) | द्वाभ्याम् (dvābhyām) | | Chaturthi (4) | द्वाभ्याम् (dvābhyām) | द्वाभ्याम् (dvābhyām) | द्वाभ्याम् (dvābhyām) | | Panchami (5) | द्वाभ्याम् (dvābhyām) | द्वाभ्याम् (dvābhyām) | द्वाभ्याम् (dvābhyām) | | Shashthi (6) | द्वयोः (dvayoḥ) | द्वयोः (dvayoḥ) | द्वयोः (dvayoḥ) | | Saptami (7) | द्वयोः (dvayoḥ) | द्वयोः (dvayoḥ) | द्वयोः (dvayoḥ) | > Cases 3, 4, and 5 merge into one form in the dual. Cases 6 and 7 merge too. The dual number compresses the case system.

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: From पञ्चन् (5) onward, numbers decline for case but are gender-neutral. They always take plural forms. पञ्चन् (pañcan, five): | Case | Form | |---|---| | Prathama (1) | पञ्च (pañca) | | Dvitiya (2) | पञ्च (pañca) | | Tritiya (3) | पञ्चभिः (pañcabhiḥ) | | Chaturthi (4) | पञ्चभ्यः (pañcabhyaḥ) | | Panchami (5) | पञ्चभ्यः (pañcabhyaḥ) | | Shashthi (6) | पञ्चानाम् (pañcānām) | | Saptami (7) | पञ्चसु (pañcasu) | The other numbers (6–10) follow the same pattern with their respective stems.

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: `` पञ्च बालाः क्रीडन्ति pañca bālāḥ krīḍanti five boys play ` Here पञ्च is in prathama vibhakti (nominative) because it describes the subject बालाः. ` त्रिषु ग्रामेषु triṣu grāmeṣu in three villages `` Both the number and the noun take saptami vibhakti (locative). The number agrees with the noun in case. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: The stem is अस्मद् (asmad), but you'll never see it bare. The declined forms are suppletive — they don't follow regular rules. You just learn them. | Case | Singular (I) | Dual (we two) | Plural (we) | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | अहम् (aham) | आवाम् (āvām) | वयम् (vayam) | | Dvitiya (2) | माम् / मा (mām / mā) | आवाम् / नौ (āvām / nau) | अस्मान् / नः (asmān / naḥ) | | Tritiya (3) | मया (mayā) | आवाभ्याम् (āvābhyām) | अस्माभिः (asmābhiḥ) | | Chaturthi (4) | मह्यम् / मे (mahyam / me) | आवाभ्याम् / नौ (āvābhyām / nau) | अस्मभ्यम् / नः (asmabhyam / naḥ) | | Panchami (5) | मत् (mat) | आवाभ्याम् (āvābhyām) | अस्मत् (asmat) | | Shashthi (6) | मम / मे (mama / me) | आवयोः / नौ (āvayoḥ / nau) | अस्माकम् / नः (asmākam / naḥ) | | Saptami (7) | मयि (mayi) | आवयोः (āvayoḥ) | अस्मासु (asmāsu) | > The short forms (मा, मे, नौ, नः) are enclitic — they attach to the preceding word and never start a sentence. They're like the unstressed "me" in English "give me that" versus the stressed "ME" in "give it to ME." > अहम् (aham) → Latin "ego" → English "I". Same PIE root *éǵh₂. The first person pronoun has survived for 5,000+ years.

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: | Case | Singular (you) | Dual (you two) | Plural (you all) | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | त्वम् (tvam) | युवाम् (yuvām) | यूयम् (yūyam) | | Dvitiya (2) | त्वाम् / त्वा (tvām / tvā) | युवाम् / वाम् (yuvām / vām) | युष्मान् / वः (yuṣmān / vaḥ) | | Tritiya (3) | त्वया (tvayā) | युवाभ्याम् (yuvābhyām) | युष्माभिः (yuṣmābhiḥ) | | Chaturthi (4) | तुभ्यम् / ते (tubhyam / te) | युवाभ्याम् / वाम् (yuvābhyām / vām) | युष्मभ्यम् / वः (yuṣmabhyam / vaḥ) | | Panchami (5) | त्वत् (tvat) | युवाभ्याम् (yuvābhyām) | युष्मत् (yuṣmat) | | Shashthi (6) | तव / ते (tava / te) | युवयोः / वाम् (yuvayoḥ / vām) | युष्माकम् / वः (yuṣmākam / vaḥ) | | Saptami (7) | त्वयि (tvayi) | युवयोः (yuvayoḥ) | युष्मासु (yuṣmāsu) | > Key pattern: first and second person pronouns have no gender. "I" and "you" don't need to declare masculine/feminine/neuter — the speaker and listener are known. Gender enters only in the third person, where you're pointing at someone/something absent. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: | Case | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | सः (saḥ) | तौ (tau) | ते (te) | | Dvitiya (2) | तम् (tam) | तौ (tau) | तान् (tān) | | Tritiya (3) | तेन (tena) | ताभ्याम् (tābhyām) | तैः (taiḥ) | | Chaturthi (4) | तस्मै (tasmai) | ताभ्याम् (tābhyām) | तेभ्यः (tebhyaḥ) | | Panchami (5) | तस्मात् (tasmāt) | ताभ्याम् (tābhyām) | तेभ्यः (tebhyaḥ) | | Shashthi (6) | तस्य (tasya) | तयोः (tayoḥ) | तेषाम् (teṣām) | | Saptami (7) | तस्मिन् (tasmin) | तयोः (tayoḥ) | तेषु (teṣu) | > तस्मात् (tasmāt, "from that / therefore") — you've seen this word. It appears constantly in the Gita and Upanishads as a logical connector: "therefore." It's just the ablative of तत्.

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: | Case | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | सा (sā) | ते (te) | ताः (tāḥ) | | Dvitiya (2) | ताम् (tām) | ते (te) | ताः (tāḥ) | | Tritiya (3) | तया (tayā) | ताभ्याम् (tābhyām) | ताभिः (tābhiḥ) | | Chaturthi (4) | तस्यै (tasyai) | ताभ्याम् (tābhyām) | ताभ्यः (tābhyaḥ) | | Panchami (5) | तस्याः (tasyāḥ) | ताभ्याम् (tābhyām) | ताभ्यः (tābhyaḥ) | | Shashthi (6) | तस्याः (tasyāḥ) | तयोः (tayoḥ) | तासाम् (tāsām) | | Saptami (7) | तस्याम् (tasyām) | तयोः (tayoḥ) | तासु (tāsu) |

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: | Case | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | तत् (tat) | ते (te) | तानि (tāni) | | Dvitiya (2) | तत् (tat) | ते (te) | तानि (tāni) | | Tritiya (3) | तेन (tena) | ताभ्याम् (tābhyām) | तैः (taiḥ) | | Chaturthi (4) | तस्मै (tasmai) | ताभ्याम् (tābhyām) | तेभ्यः (tebhyaḥ) | | Panchami (5) | तस्मात् (tasmāt) | ताभ्याम् (tābhyām) | तेभ्यः (tebhyaḥ) | | Shashthi (6) | तस्य (tasya) | तयोः (tayoḥ) | तेषाम् (teṣām) | | Saptami (7) | तस्मिन् (tasmin) | तयोः (tayoḥ) | तेषु (teṣu) | > Neuter differs from masculine only in cases 1 and 2. Cases 3–7 are identical. This holds across most Sanskrit declensions — the neuter "splits off" only in the nominative and accusative. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: एतत् = ए (e) + तत् (tat) → adds a prefix to the तत् forms. The declension of एतत् follows तत् exactly, with ए prefixed: | | Masculine Sg. | Feminine Sg. | Neuter Sg. | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | एषः (eṣaḥ) | एषा (eṣā) | एतत् (etat) | | Dvitiya (2) | एतम् (etam) | एताम् (etām) | एतत् (etat) | | Tritiya (3) | एतेन (etena) | एतया (etayā) | एतेन (etena) | | Shashthi (6) | एतस्य (etasya) | एतस्याः (etasyāḥ) | एतस्य (etasya) | The rest follows the same prefix pattern. If you know तत्, you know एतत्.

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: इदम् is the other "this" pronoun. It's more common and more irregular. Masculine: | Case | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | अयम् (ayam) | इमौ (imau) | इमे (ime) | | Dvitiya (2) | इमम् (imam) | इमौ (imau) | इमान् (imān) | | Tritiya (3) | अनेन (anena) | आभ्याम् (ābhyām) | एभिः (ebhiḥ) | | Chaturthi (4) | अस्मै (asmai) | आभ्याम् (ābhyām) | एभ्यः (ebhyaḥ) | | Panchami (5) | अस्मात् (asmāt) | आभ्याम् (ābhyām) | एभ्यः (ebhyaḥ) | | Shashthi (6) | अस्य (asya) | अनयोः (anayoḥ) | एषाम् (eṣām) | | Saptami (7) | अस्मिन् (asmin) | अनयोः (anayoḥ) | एषु (eṣu) | Feminine: | Case | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | इयम् (iyam) | इमे (ime) | इमाः (imāḥ) | | Dvitiya (2) | इमाम् (imām) | इमे (ime) | इमाः (imāḥ) | | Tritiya (3) | अनया (anayā) | आभ्याम् (ābhyām) | आभिः (ābhiḥ) | | Chaturthi (4) | अस्यै (asyai) | आभ्याम् (ābhyām) | आभ्यः (ābhyaḥ) | | Panchami (5) | अस्याः (asyāḥ) | आभ्याम् (ābhyām) | आभ्यः (ābhyaḥ) | | Shashthi (6) | अस्याः (asyāḥ) | अनयोः (anayoḥ) | आसाम् (āsām) | | Saptami (7) | अस्याम् (asyām) | अनयोः (anayoḥ) | आसु (āsu) | Neuter: | Case | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | इदम् (idam) | इमे (ime) | इमानि (imāni) | | Dvitiya (2) | इदम् (idam) | इमे (ime) | इमानि (imāni) | Cases 3–7 neuter = same as masculine (same pattern as तत्).

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: | Pronoun | Meaning | Distance | |---|---|---| | इदम् (idam) | this | Near speaker — what I'm holding, what's here | | एतत् (etat) | this | Near speaker — slightly more emphatic/specific | | तत् (tat) | that | Away from speaker — what's over there, what was mentioned | > Compare: English has two (this/that). Sanskrit has three. Japanese has three too (kore/sore/are), split by speaker-listener-neither. Sanskrit's split is subtler — इदम् vs एतत् is about emphasis, not spatial distance. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: | Case | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | कः (kaḥ) | कौ (kau) | के (ke) | | Dvitiya (2) | कम् (kam) | कौ (kau) | कान् (kān) | | Tritiya (3) | केन (kena) | काभ्याम् (kābhyām) | कैः (kaiḥ) | | Chaturthi (4) | कस्मै (kasmai) | काभ्याम् (kābhyām) | केभ्यः (kebhyaḥ) | | Panchami (5) | कस्मात् (kasmāt) | काभ्याम् (kābhyām) | केभ्यः (kebhyaḥ) | | Shashthi (6) | कस्य (kasya) | कयोः (kayoḥ) | केषाम् (keṣām) | | Saptami (7) | कस्मिन् (kasmin) | कयोः (kayoḥ) | केषु (keṣu) |

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: | Case | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | का (kā) | के (ke) | काः (kāḥ) | | Dvitiya (2) | काम् (kām) | के (ke) | काः (kāḥ) | | Tritiya (3) | कया (kayā) | काभ्याम् (kābhyām) | काभिः (kābhiḥ) | | Shashthi (6) | कस्याः (kasyāḥ) | कयोः (kayoḥ) | कासाम् (kāsām) |

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: | Case | Singular | Dual | Plural | |---|---|---|---| | Prathama (1) | किम् (kim) | के (ke) | कानि (kāni) | | Dvitiya (2) | किम् (kim) | के (ke) | कानि (kāni) | Cases 3–7 neuter = same as masculine. > Pattern alert: compare तत् and किम् side by side. Replace त with क in nearly every form: तस्मै → कस्मै, तेन → केन, तस्य → कस्य. The demonstrative and interrogative are structural mirrors. Panini would call this predictable — the pronominal declension is a single template, swapping stems.

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: किम् is also a building block: | Compound | Breakdown | Meaning | |---|---|---| | किमर्थम् (kimartham) | किम् + अर्थम् | for what purpose? / why? | | कुत्र (kutra) | कु (from किम्) + त्र | where? | | कदा (kadā) | क + दा | when? | | कथम् (katham) | क + थम् | how? | | किमपि (kimapi) | किम् + अपि | something (indefinite) | > Add अपि (api) to any किम्-form and it becomes indefinite: कः (who?) → कोऽपि (koʼpi, someone). Add न and it becomes negative: न कोऽपि (na koʼpi, no one). A single system generates questions, indefinites, and negatives. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: Consider the English sentence: "Give it to her." - "it" — no case marking (object only by position), no gender, no number distinction - "her" — encodes gender (feminine) and case (dative), but not number Now the Sanskrit equivalent: `` तत् तस्यै देहि tat tasyai dehi that(neuter/accusative/singular) her(feminine/dative/singular) give(imperative/2nd-person/singular) ` Every word carries its full identity: | Word | Encodes | |---|---| | तत् (tat) | neuter gender + accusative case + singular number | | तस्यै (tasyai) | feminine gender + dative case + singular number | | देहि (dehi) | imperative mood + second person + singular number | Three words. Nine pieces of information. No word-order dependency. You could rearrange them any way and the meaning wouldn't change: ` तस्यै तत् देहि = देहि तत् तस्यै = तत् देहि तस्यै `` All identical in meaning. In English, you need word order + prepositions + context to recover the same information. In Sanskrit, the morphology does the work.

metadata.section: Part VII: Pronouns Encode More Than English
metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: | Feature | English | Sanskrit | |---|---|---| | Forms to memorize | Few (~5 pronoun forms per person) | Many (~21 forms per pronoun per gender) | | Word order | Fixed (SVO) — meaning depends on position | Free — meaning is in the morphology | | Ambiguity | Common ("They saw her duck" — ?) | Rare — each form is explicit | | Compression | Low — needs helper words (to, for, by, of) | High — one word = gender + case + number | ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: Parse each sentence — identify the pronoun form, its case, gender, and number: 1. > अहं पुस्तकं पठामि > ahaṃ pustakaṃ paṭhāmi > I book read > "I read a book." - अहम् → prathama, singular, first person (the doer) - पुस्तकम् → dvitiya, neuter, singular (the thing read) - पठामि → present tense, first person, singular 2. > सः तस्यै पुष्पं ददाति > saḥ tasyai puṣpaṃ dadāti > he to-her flower gives > "He gives a flower to her." - सः → prathama, masculine, singular (he — the doer) - तस्यै → chaturthi, feminine, singular (to/for her — the recipient) - पुष्पम् → dvitiya, neuter, singular (the flower — the thing given) - ददाति → present tense, third person, singular 3. > के त्वां पृच्छन्ति? > ke tvāṃ pṛcchanti? > who(pl) you ask? > "Who (all) are asking you?" - के → prathama, masculine, plural (who — the doers, plural) - त्वाम् → dvitiya, singular, second person (you — the one asked) - पृच्छन्ति → present tense, third person, plural 4. > एषां पञ्चानां बालानां मध्ये अयं श्रेष्ठः > eṣāṃ pañcānāṃ bālānāṃ madhye ayaṃ śreṣṭhaḥ > of-these five boys among this-one best > "Among these five boys, this one is the best." - एषाम् → shashthi, masculine, plural (of these) - पञ्चानाम् → shashthi, plural (of five) — number declines in same case as noun! - बालानाम् → shashthi, masculine, plural (of boys) - मध्ये → saptami (locative — "in the middle / among") - अयम् → prathama, masculine, singular (this one — the subject) - श्रेष्ठः → prathama, masculine, singular (best — adjective agrees with subject) 5. > किं तव नाम? > kiṃ tava nāma? > what your name? > "What is your name?" - किम् → prathama, neuter, singular (what) - तव → shashthi, singular, second person (your) - नाम → prathama, neuter, singular (name)

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: Try forming these in Sanskrit: 1. "We go to the village." (hint: वयम् + ग्रामम् + गच्छामः) 2. "Whose book is this?" (hint: कस्य + पुस्तकम् + इदम्) 3. "She speaks to them." (hint: सा + तेभ्यः + वदति) 4. "By whom was this done?" (hint: केन + इदम् + कृतम्) 5. "I give two fruits to you." (hint: अहम् + द्वे + फले + तुभ्यम् + ददामि) ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: After this lesson, you can: - Count 1–10 in Sanskrit and understand that numbers are not static labels — they decline - Use first and second person pronouns in all cases and numbers - Use तत् (that/he/she/it) — the most important pronoun — in all three genders, eight cases, three numbers - Distinguish "this" (इदम्/एतत्) from "that" (तत्) - Ask questions using किम् and build question words from it - Understand why a single Sanskrit pronoun form carries more information than an entire English prepositional phrase ---

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: Vibhakti lesson (03): the case endings you learned for nouns now apply to pronouns and numbers — the system is uniform - Lakara lesson (04): verb forms encode person and number, so with pronouns you now have subject-verb agreement working in both directions - Sandhi lesson (02): when pronouns meet other words at sentence boundaries, sandhi rules apply — सः + अस्ति → सोऽस्ति (saḥ + asti → so'sti)

metadata.lesson_title: Sankhya & Sarvanama — Numbers and Pronouns

text: This is not an exaggeration. You know the sounds (varnamala), how they merge (sandhi), how nouns declare their roles (vibhakti), how verbs encode tense and person (lakaras), and how numbers and pronouns work. That is everything you need to decode real Sanskrit verse. This lesson is where it all clicks. We take actual shlokas — lines that have been chanted for thousands of years — and take them apart, bolt by bolt. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: श्लोक (śloka) — from the dhatu श्लोक् (ślok, to compose verse) The standard shloka uses the अनुष्टुभ् छन्दस् (anuṣṭubh chandas) meter: | Property | Value | |---|---| | Padas (quarters) | 4 | | Syllables per pada | 8 | | Total syllables | 32 | | Structure | 2 lines, each with 2 padas | `` Line 1: ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ | ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ pada 1 (8) pada 2 (8) Line 2: ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ | ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ pada 3 (8) pada 4 (8) `` This is by far the most common meter in Sanskrit literature. The Mahabharata (~100,000 shlokas), most of the Bhagavad Gita, the Ramayana, Subhashitas — all primarily anushtubh. > Why 32 syllables? The anushtubh sits in the sweet spot — long enough for a complete thought, short enough to memorize. It is the iambic pentameter of Sanskrit. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: छन्दस् (chandas) = meter, from छद् (chad, to cover/please) — "that which covers speech with rhythm" Sanskrit verse isn't just counted by syllables. Each syllable is classified: | Type | Sanskrit | Symbol | Rule | |---|---|---|---| | Light (laghu) | लघु | ◡ | Short vowel not followed by conjunct consonant | | Heavy (guru) | गुरु | — | Long vowel, or short vowel followed by 2+ consonants | In anushtubh, the key constraint is in padas 2 and 4 (the even quarters): - 5th syllable must be laghu (◡) - 6th syllable must be guru (—) - 7th syllable in pada 2: guru (—) - 7th syllable in pada 4: laghu (◡) This is enough to get started. Chandas is its own deep subject — Pingala's Chandahshastra catalogs dozens of meters — but recognizing anushtubh gets you through 80% of what you will read. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: Every time you approach a shloka, follow this sequence: `` 1. Read aloud — let the sound register first 2. Split sandhi — break merged words back apart 3. Identify vibhakti — tag each noun's case 4. Find the verb — this is the anchor of the sentence 5. Reconstruct — who did what, to whom, where, how? `` Sanskrit word order is free (vibhakti handles grammar), so poets arrange words for sound and rhythm, not for parsing convenience. You have to actively reconstruct the prose order. This is the skill. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: | Word | Devanagari | Root / Form | Case | Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | dharmakṣetre | धर्मक्षेत्रे | dharma + kṣetra (field) | Saptami (locative, singular) | in the field of dharma | | kurukṣetre | कुरुक्षेत्रे | kuru + kṣetra | Saptami (locative, singular) | in the field of the Kurus | | samavetāḥ | समवेताः | sam + ava + √इ (i, to go) → samaveta (assembled) | Prathama (nominative, plural) | [those who] assembled | | yuyutsavaḥ | युयुत्सवः | √युध् (yudh, to fight) → yuyutsu (desiring to fight, desiderative) | Prathama (nominative, plural) | desiring to fight | | māmakāḥ | मामकाः | mama (my) + -ka (suffix) → māmaka (mine, my people) | Prathama (nominative, plural) | my [sons] | | pāṇḍavāḥ | पाण्डवाः | pāṇḍu + -va (patronymic) → sons of Pandu | Prathama (nominative, plural) | the Pandavas | | ca | च | conjunction | — | and | | eva | एव | particle | — | indeed / also | | kim | किम् | interrogative pronoun | — | what? | | akurvata | अकुर्वत | √कृ (kṛ, to do), laṅ-lakāra (past tense), ātmanepada, prathama puruṣa, bahuvacana | — | did they do? | | sañjaya | सञ्जय | proper name | Sambodhana (vocative) | O Sanjaya! |

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: In the field of dharma, in the field of the Kurus, assembled and desiring to fight — my sons and the Pandavas — what did they do, O Sanjaya? Note the structure: two locatives set the scene, then nominatives identify the actors, then the verb-question arrives at the end. Dhritarashtra is blind — he cannot see the battlefield. This is the first line of 700 verses. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: | Written | Split | Rule | |---|---|---| | पूर्णमदः | पूर्णम् + अदः | m + a → ma (simple joining) | | पूर्णमिदम् | पूर्णम् + इदम् | m + i → mi | | पूर्णमुदच्यते | पूर्णम् + उदच्यते | m + u → mu | | पूर्णमादाय | पूर्णम् + आदाय | m + ā → mā | | पूर्णमेवावशिष्यते | पूर्णम् + एव + अवशिष्यते | m + e → me; a + a → ā (dīrgha sandhi) |

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: | Word | Root / Form | Case | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | पूर्णम् (pūrṇam) | √पॄ (pṝ, to fill) → pūrṇa (full, complete) | Prathama (nom, neuter singular) | that which is full/complete | | अदः (adaḥ) | demonstrative pronoun (that,远 — the distant one) | Prathama (nom, neuter singular) | that (invisible, beyond) | | इदम् (idam) | demonstrative pronoun (this, 近 — the near one) | Prathama (nom, neuter singular) | this (visible, here) | | पूर्णात् (pūrṇāt) | pūrṇa | Panchami (ablative, singular) | from the full | | उदच्यते (udacyate) | उद् (ud, upward) + √चि (ci, to gather) → passive: "is produced/arises" | Verb: laṭ, karmavācya, prathama puruṣa, eka. | arises, is produced | | पूर्णस्य (pūrṇasya) | pūrṇa | Shashthi (genitive, singular) | of the full | | आदाय (ādāya) | आ + √दा (dā, to give) → ādā (to take away), lyap-kṛdanta (absolutive) | — | having taken away | | अवशिष्यते (avaśiṣyate) | अव + √शिष् (śiṣ, to remain) → passive: "remains" | Verb: laṭ, karmavācya, prathama puruṣa, eka. | remains | | एव (eva) | particle | — | only, indeed |

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: That is full. This is full. From the full, the full arises. > Having taken the full from the full, the full alone remains. One word — पूर्ण — used 6 times. The verse is a meditation on infinity: if you subtract infinity from infinity, infinity remains. This is the invocation of the Isha Upanishad, recited before the teaching begins. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: ॐ भूर्भुवः स्वः > तत्सवितुर्वरेण्यं भर्गो देवस्य धीमहि । > धियो यो नः प्रचोदयात् ॥ oṃ bhūr bhuvaḥ svaḥ tat savitur vareṇyaṃ bhargo devasya dhīmahi | dhiyo yo naḥ pracodayāt || The Gayatri is from Rigveda 3.62.10 (attributed to Rishi Vishvamitra). It uses the gāyatrī chandas — 3 padas of 8 syllables each (24 total), distinct from the standard anushtubh.

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: ॐ भूर्भुवः स्वः (oṃ bhūr bhuvaḥ svaḥ) — these are not part of the shloka proper. They are the three व्याहृतियः (vyāhṛtayaḥ), prefixed as an invocation: | Vyahriti | Root | Meaning | |---|---|---| | भूः (bhūḥ) | √भू (bhū, to be) | Earth / physical plane | | भुवः (bhuvaḥ) | √भू (bhū) | Atmosphere / intermediate plane | | स्वः (svaḥ) | √सु (su, well/good) or sva (self) | Heaven / celestial plane |

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: | Written | Split | Rule | |---|---|---| | तत्सवितुर्वरेण्यम् | तत् + सवितुः + वरेण्यम् | t + s → ts; visarga before v → r (ruki) | | भर्गो | भर्गः + [next vowel triggers visarga → o] | visarga sandhi | | धियो | धियः + [यो] | visarga sandhi (aḥ before voiced → o) |

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: | Word | Root / Form | Case | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | तत् (tat) | demonstrative pronoun | Dvitiya (accusative, neuter singular) | that | | सवितुः (savituḥ) | √सू (sū, to impel/generate) → सवितृ (savitṛ, the impeller — the Sun) | Shashthi (genitive, singular) | of Savitr (the sun as creative force) | | वरेण्यम् (vareṇyam) | √वृ (vṛ, to choose) → vareṇya (worthy of choosing, most excellent) | Dvitiya (acc, neuter singular) | most excellent, worthy of worship | | भर्गः (bhargaḥ) | √भ्रज् (bhraj, to shine) → bhargas (radiance, splendour) | Dvitiya (acc, neuter singular) | radiance, effulgence | | देवस्य (devasya) | √दिव् (div, to shine) → deva (the shining one, deity) | Shashthi (genitive, singular) | of the deity | | धीमहि (dhīmahi) | √धी (dhī, to meditate/contemplate), āśīrliṅ-lakāra / vidhiliṅ, first person plural | Verb | may we meditate upon | | धियः (dhiyaḥ) | √धी (dhī) → dhī (intellect, thought) | Dvitiya (acc, plural) | [our] intellects / thoughts | | यः (yaḥ) | relative pronoun | Prathama (nom, masculine singular) | who | | नः (naḥ) | अस्मद् (asmad, we/us), enclitic form | Shashthi (genitive, plural) | our | | प्रचोदयात् (pracodayāt) | प्र + √चुद् (cud, to impel) → causal form pracoday (to inspire/stimulate), āśīrliṅ / vidhiliṅ | Verb | may [he] inspire/impel |

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: The natural prose order: > (यः) सवितुः देवस्य तत् वरेण्यं भर्गः धीमहि, (सः) नः धियः प्रचोदयात् > May we meditate upon that most excellent radiance of the divine Savitr; may he inspire our intellects. Two verbs, two requests: (1) may we meditate on the light; (2) may that light sharpen our minds. That is all it says. No mysticism added — just a request for clarity. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: | Written | Split | Rule | |---|---|---| | विनयाद्याति | विनयात् + याति | t + y → dy (voice assimilation) | | पात्रत्वाद्धनम् | पात्रत्वात् + धनम् | t + dh → ddh (voice assimilation) | | धनाद्धर्मम् | धनात् + धर्मम् | t + dh → ddh | | आप्नोति | आ + √आप् (āp, to obtain) + नोति | no sandhi — this is morphological |

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: | Word | Root / Form | Case | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | विद्या (vidyā) | √विद् (vid, to know) → vidyā (knowledge) | Prathama (nom, fem. singular) | knowledge | | ददाति (dadāti) | √दा (dā, to give), laṭ, parasmaipada, 3rd singular | Verb | gives | | विनयम् (vinayam) | वि + √नी (nī, to lead) → vinaya (humility, discipline) | Dvitiya (acc, singular) | humility | | विनयात् (vinayāt) | vinaya | Panchami (ablative, singular) | from humility | | याति (yāti) | √या (yā, to go/attain), laṭ, 3rd singular | Verb | one attains | | पात्रताम् (pātratām) | pātra (vessel/worthy recipient) + -tā (abstract suffix: "-ness") | Dvitiya (acc, singular) | worthiness | | पात्रत्वात् (pātratvāt) | pātra + -tva (abstract suffix, same as -tā) | Panchami (ablative, singular) | from worthiness | | धनम् (dhanam) | √धा (dhā, to hold/place) → dhana (wealth) | Dvitiya (acc, singular) | wealth | | आप्नोति (āpnoti) | आ + √आप् (āp, to obtain), laṭ, 3rd singular | Verb | one obtains | | धनात् (dhanāt) | dhana | Panchami (ablative, singular) | from wealth | | धर्मम् (dharmam) | √धृ (dhṛ, to hold/sustain) → dharma | Dvitiya (acc, singular) | dharma (right action) | | ततः (tataḥ) | adverb | — | from that, then | | सुखम् (sukham) | su (good) + kha (space/axle-hole) → sukha (ease, happiness) | Dvitiya (acc, singular) | happiness |

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: Knowledge gives humility. From humility, one attains worthiness. > From worthiness, one obtains wealth. From wealth, dharma. From dharma, happiness. The chain: विद्या → विनय → पात्रता → धन → धर्म → सुख. Five ablatives (from X comes Y) build a causal cascade. Notice every pada has its own subject-verb-object, and the ablative is the link between them. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: या कुन्देन्दुतुषारहारधवला या शुभ्रवस्त्रावृता > या वीणावरदण्डमण्डितकरा या श्वेतपद्मासना । > या ब्रह्माच्युतशङ्करप्रभृतिभिर्देवैः सदा वन्दिता > सा मां पातु सरस्वती भगवती निःशेषजाड्यापहा ॥ yā kundendutuṣārahāradhavalā yā śubhravastrāvṛtā yā vīṇāvaradaṇḍamaṇḍitakarā yā śvetapadmāsanā | yā brahmācyutaśaṅkaraprabhṛtibhirdevaiḥ sadā vanditā sā māṃ pātu sarasvatī bhagavatī niḥśeṣajāḍyāpahā || This verse is not anushtubh — it uses the शार्दूलविक्रीडित (śārdūlavikrīḍita, "tiger's sport") meter, 19 syllables per pada. A longer, more ornate meter for formal invocations.

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: | Written | Split | Rule | |---|---|---| | कुन्देन्दुतुषारहारधवला | कुन्द + इन्दु + तुषार + हार + धवला | compound (no sandhi — this is a samāsa, a compound word) | | शुभ्रवस्त्रावृता | शुभ्र + वस्त्र + आवृता | compound | | वीणावरदण्डमण्डितकरा | वीणा + वर + दण्ड + मण्डित + करा | compound | | ब्रह्माच्युतशङ्करप्रभृतिभिः | ब्रह्मा + अच्युत + शङ्कर + प्रभृति + भिः | compound + instrumental plural ending | | निःशेषजाड्यापहा | निः + शेष + जाड्य + अपहा | niḥ (without) + śeṣa (remainder) + jāḍya (dullness) + apahā (remover) |

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: | Word | Root / Compound | Case | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | या (yā) | relative pronoun (feminine) | Prathama (nom, fem. sing.) | she who | | कुन्देन्दुतुषारहारधवला | kunda (jasmine) + indu (moon) + tuṣāra (frost) + hāra (garland) + dhavalā (white) | Prathama (nom) | white as jasmine, moon, frost, and pearl-garlands | | शुभ्रवस्त्रावृता | śubhra (bright) + vastra (garment) + āvṛtā (covered) | Prathama (nom) | draped in bright garments | | वीणावरदण्डमण्डितकरा | vīṇā + vara (excellent) + daṇḍa (stem/neck) + maṇḍita (adorned) + karā (hand) | Prathama (nom) | whose hand is adorned with the fine neck of a veena | | श्वेतपद्मासना | śveta (white) + padma (lotus) + āsanā (seated) | Prathama (nom) | seated on a white lotus | | प्रभृतिभिः (prabhṛtibhiḥ) | pra + √bhṛ (bhṛ, to bear) → prabhṛti (beginning with, et cetera) | Tritiya (instrumental) | beginning with, led by | | देवैः (devaiḥ) | deva | Tritiya (instrumental, plural) | by the gods | | सदा (sadā) | adverb | — | always | | वन्दिता (vanditā) | √वन्द् (vand, to praise/bow to), past passive participle | Prathama (nom) | praised, worshipped | | सा (sā) | demonstrative pronoun (fem.) | Prathama (nom) | she | | माम् (mām) | अस्मद् (I/me) | Dvitiya (acc) | me | | पातु (pātu) | √पा (pā, to protect), loṭ-lakāra (imperative), 3rd singular | Verb | may she protect | | सरस्वती (sarasvatī) | saras (flow/lake) + -vatī (possessing) | Prathama (nom) | Saraswati — "she who possesses flow" | | भगवती (bhagavatī) | bhaga (glory/fortune) + -vat + feminine -ī | Prathama (nom) | the divine one | | निःशेषजाड्यापहा | complete remover of all dullness (see split above) | Prathama (nom) | she who removes all traces of stupidity |

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: She who is white as jasmine, moon, frost, and pearl-garlands; she who is draped in bright garments; she whose hand is adorned with the fine neck of a veena; she who is seated on a white lotus; she who is always worshipped by Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva, and the other gods — may that Saraswati, that divine one, the complete remover of all dullness, protect me. The entire shloka is one sentence. Four या (yā, "she who...") clauses build suspense, stacking descriptions. Then सा (sā, "she") resolves the sentence with a single verb: पातु (may she protect). This is the relative-correlative construction — या...सा — one of Sanskrit's most characteristic sentence patterns. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: | Written | Split | Rule | |---|---|---| | असतो | असतः | visarga sandhi (aḥ → o before voiced) | | सद्गमय | सत् + गमय | t + g → dg (voice assimilation) | | तमसो | तमसः | same visarga sandhi | | ज्योतिर्गमय | ज्योतिः + गमय | visarga → r before voiced consonant | | मृत्योर्मा | मृत्योः + मा | visarga → r before m |

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: | Word | Root / Form | Case | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | असतः (asataḥ) | a (not) + √अस् (as, to be) → asat (unreal, non-being) | Panchami (ablative, singular) | from the unreal | | मा (mā) | अस्मद् (me), enclitic accusative | Dvitiya (acc) | me | | सत् (sat) | √अस् (as, to be) → sat (real, being, truth) | — (adverbial/accusative goal) | to the real | | गमय (gamaya) | √गम् (gam, to go), causal (णिच्) imperative, 2nd person singular | Verb | lead! (cause to go) | | तमसः (tamasaḥ) | √तम् (tam, to be exhausted/dark) → tamas (darkness) | Panchami (ablative) | from darkness | | ज्योतिः (jyotiḥ) | √ज्युत् (jyut, to shine) → jyotis (light) | — (goal) | to light | | मृत्योः (mṛtyoḥ) | √मृ (mṛ, to die) → mṛtyu (death) | Panchami (ablative) | from death | | अमृतम् (amṛtam) | a (not) + mṛta (dead) → amṛta (deathless, immortal) | — (goal) | to immortality |

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: From the unreal, lead me to the real. > From darkness, lead me to light. > From death, lead me to immortality. Three lines, one structure: ablative + मा + goal + गमय. The verb is causative — not "let me go" but "make me go." This is not a passive wish. It is a direct instruction to the teacher (or the divine): move me from here to there. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: Sandhi splits: - सिध्यन्ति ← no split needed (√सिध् + यन्ति) - कार्याणि ← no split needed (कार्य + plural neuter nom.) - मनोरथैः ← मनः + रथैः (visarga sandhi: aḥ + r → o) - सुप्तस्य ← no split (√स्वप् → supta, past participle + genitive) - प्रविशन्ति ← प्र + √विश् + अन्ति Word-by-word: | Word | Root | Case | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | उद्यमेन (udyamena) | उद् + √यम् (yam, to restrain/exert) → udyama (effort) | Tritiya (instrumental) | by effort | | हि (hi) | particle | — | indeed | | सिध्यन्ति (sidhyanti) | √सिध् (sidh, to succeed), laṭ, 3rd plural | Verb | succeed, are accomplished | | कार्याणि (kāryāṇi) | √कृ (kṛ, to do) → kārya (that which is to be done, task) | Prathama (nom, neuter plural) | tasks | | न (na) | — | — | not | | मनोरथैः (manorathaiḥ) | manas (mind) + ratha (chariot) → manoratha (desire, daydream — "mind-chariot") | Tritiya (instrumental, plural) | by daydreams | | सुप्तस्य (suptasya) | √स्वप् (svap, to sleep) → supta (sleeping) | Shashthi (genitive, singular) | of a sleeping | | सिंहस्य (siṃhasya) | siṃha (lion) | Shashthi (genitive, singular) | lion's | | प्रविशन्ति (praviśanti) | प्र + √विश् (viś, to enter), laṭ, 3rd plural | Verb | enter | | मुखे (mukhe) | mukha (mouth) | Saptami (locative, singular) | in the mouth | | मृगाः (mṛgāḥ) | √मृग् (mṛg, to seek) → mṛga (deer, prey) | Prathama (nom, plural) | deer/prey | Translation: > Tasks are accomplished by effort, not by daydreams. Deer do not enter the mouth of a sleeping lion.

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: Sandhi splits: - तथैव ← तथा + एव (ā + e → ai, vṛddhi sandhi) - धर्महिंसा ← this is a compound: dharma + hiṃsā (violence for dharma's sake) Word-by-word: | Word | Root | Case | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | अहिंसा (ahiṃsā) | a (not) + √हिंस् (hiṃs, to harm) → ahiṃsā (non-violence) | Prathama (nom) | non-violence | | परमः (paramaḥ) | parama (highest, supreme) | Prathama (nom) | the highest | | धर्मः (dharmaḥ) | √धृ (dhṛ) → dharma | Prathama (nom) | dharma | | धर्महिंसा (dharmahiṃsā) | dharma + hiṃsā (violence in service of dharma) | Prathama (nom) | righteous [use of] force | | तथा (tathā) | adverb | — | likewise, so too | | एव (eva) | particle | — | indeed | | च (ca) | — | — | and | Translation: > Non-violence is the highest dharma. And so too is righteous force. This is the full verse often cut short. The first half alone is famous. The second half complicates it — sometimes dharma requires force. The verse does not resolve the tension. It holds both. ---

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: After six lessons, you have: | Tool | Lesson | What It Does | |---|---|---| | Varnamala | 01 | Read any Sanskrit sound | | Sandhi | 02 | Split merged words apart | | Vibhakti | 03 | Identify every noun's grammatical role | | Lakaras | 04 | Identify verb tense, person, and number | | Numbers & Pronouns | 05 | Handle quantifiers and references | | Shloka reading | 06 | Combine all the above to read real verse | The method is mechanical: split → tag → find verb → reconstruct. The more shlokas you read, the faster the pattern recognition gets. Start with anushtubh verses (they are everywhere), and the sandhi splits will start happening in your head. Phase 1 is done. You can read Sanskrit.

metadata.lesson_title: Reading Shlokas — Lesson 6

text: The ईशोपनिषद् (Īśopaniṣad) is the shortest of the major Upanishads — eighteen verses. It is also, arguably, the most compressed. Every central tension of Vedantic philosophy appears here: action versus renunciation, knowledge versus ignorance, the one versus the many, immanence versus transcendence. And it resolves all of them — not by picking sides, but by insisting that both poles are simultaneously true. This is your first primary text. Everything before this — the Sanskrit lessons, the Vedic overview, the philosophical scaffolding — was preparation for reading this. ---

metadata.lesson_title: The Isha Upanishad — The Whole Philosophy in Eighteen Verses

text: The Isha Upanishad belongs to the शुक्ल यजुर्वेद (Śukla Yajurveda, the "White" Yajurveda) — specifically, it is the 40th and final chapter (अध्याय, adhyāya) of the वाजसनेयि संहिता (Vājasaneyī Saṃhitā). This placement matters: it sits at the very end of the Samhita layer, at the boundary where hymns and formulas begin their transition toward philosophy. It is, structurally, a hinge. | Property | Detail | |---|---| | Parent Veda | Shukla Yajurveda | | Position | Chapter 40 of the Vajasaneyi Samhita | | Verse count | 18 (some recensions count 17) | | Meter | Mixed — mostly anushtubh (32 syllables), some trishtubh (44 syllables) | | Classification | Mukhya Upanishad (principal — one of the ~12 that Shankara commented on) | | Also called | ईशावास्योपनिषद् (Īśāvāsyopaniṣad) — from its opening words |

metadata.lesson_title: The Isha Upanishad — The Whole Philosophy in Eighteen Verses

text: ईश (īśa) — from the dhatu ईश् (īś, "to rule, to be master of, to be capable") | Derivative | Meaning | Construction | |---|---|---| | ईश (īśa) | lord, ruler | īś + a (कर्तृ-प्रत्यय, agent suffix) | | ईश्वर (īśvara) | the Lord, God | īś + vara (suffix indicating capacity) | | महेश (maheśa) | the great lord (Shiva) | mahā + īśa | | ईशान (īśāna) | ruling, presiding | present participle of īś | The Upanishad is named after its first word: ईशा (īśā), the instrumental singular of ईश — "by the Lord" or "by the ruler." The title tells you the thesis: everything is pervaded by the ruling principle. ---

metadata.lesson_title: The Isha Upanishad — The Whole Philosophy in Eighteen Verses

text: This is one of the most famous verses in all of Sanskrit literature. If you understand this verse completely, you have the core of the Isha Upanishad. The remaining seventeen verses are elaboration. > ईशावास्यमिदं सर्वं यत्किञ्च जगत्यां जगत् । > तेन त्यक्तेन भुञ्जीथा मा गृधः कस्यस्विद्धनम् ॥ १ ॥ > > īśāvāsyam idaṃ sarvaṃ yat kiñca jagatyāṃ jagat > tena tyaktena bhuñjīthā mā gṛdhaḥ kasya svid dhanam

metadata.lesson_title: The Isha Upanishad — The Whole Philosophy in Eighteen Verses

text: The verse as chanted is a continuous stream with sandhi applied. Here it is broken apart: | Compounded Form | Sandhi Split | Rule | |---|---|---| | ईशावास्यम् (īśāvāsyam) | ईशा + आवास्यम् (īśā + āvāsyam) | आ + आ → आ (savarna-dīrgha sandhi) | | यत्किञ्च (yat kiñca) | यत् + किञ्च (yat + kiñca) | No external sandhi; त् retained before क् | | जगत्यां (jagatyām) | जगत्याम् (jagatyām) | Locative singular of जगती (jagatī) | | भुञ्जीथाः (bhuñjīthāḥ) | भुञ्जीथाः | No split — single verb form | | कस्यस्विद्धनम् (kasya svid dhanam) | कस्य + स्वित् + धनम् (kasya + svit + dhanam) | त् + ध → द्ध (voiced assimilation) |
